items.prompt: Which statement best defines crowdsourcing in this part?
choices: Running all labeling only on a single on-site expert
Obtaining input or labor by soliciting a large online community
Storing petabytes solely in a relational database
Encrypting sensor packets at the edge only
items.explain: Crowdsourcing solicits contributions from a large group, typically online, for tasks a small team cannot finish alone.

items.prompt: Crowd labor often breaks work into microtasks such as labeling, transcription, or categorization.
items.explain: The taxonomy in Section 9.2.1 lists crowd labor as outsourcing decomposable microtasks.

items.prompt: Example 9.9 (Netflix Prize) primarily illustrates which use of crowdsourcing?
choices: Open contest–style innovation for algorithmic improvement
items.explain: The prize invited external teams to improve recommendations under a shared benchmark and reward.

items.prompt: How does Example 9.10 use microtasking for catalog images?
items.rubric: Mention splitting tagging into small units distributed to many workers for faster annotation.
items.sample_answer: Product-image tagging is split into microtasks (for example category labels) and distributed so thousands of items can be annotated in hours.

items.prompt: Crowdsourcing is used only for simple data entry and never for innovation contests.
items.explain: The part covers both microtask labor and innovation contests such as the Netflix Prize.
